source_metadata.title_raw: Lan George - A-19-076 - June 12, 2019 - Arroyo Grande

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Lan George 
Arroyo Grande City Council 
501 Castillo 
Arroyo Grande, CA 93420 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-076 
Dear Mr. George: 
June 12, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
As an Arroyo Grande City Councilmember, may you take part in a governmental decision to 
formally support or oppose a neighboring city's development of a homeless center, given that you 
own residential property approximately 638 feet from the potential site of the project? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes, because the Arroyo Grande City Council's formal support or opposition to a 
neighboring town's homeless center would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on 
your real property interest. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
FileNo. A-19-076 
Page No. 2 
You are a member of the Arroyo Grande City Council. A church property located in a 
neighboring city, Grover Beach, is being considered as the site of a potential homeless center. 
Neighbors of the church are upset and want the Arroyo Grande City Council to take a position 
against supporting the homeless center. The governmental decision of whether to develop a 
homeless center at that site is not the Arroyo Grande City Council's to make, however, given that 
the property is located in Grover Beach. You own a residential property approximately 638 feet 
from the church property that may become the site of the Grover Beach homeless center. 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 87100, "[ n ]o public officiaf at any level of state or local government shall 
make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a. 
gove~ental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." A 
public official has a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on certain 
enumerated economic interests, such as "[a]ny real property in which the public official has a direct 
or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (Section 87103(b).) 
With respect to the reasonable foreseeability of a financial effect on an economic interest 
not explicitly involved in a governmental decision, the financial effect need not be likely to be 
considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial 
result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's 
control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) In determining if a financial effect 
is foreseeable, "[t]he extent to which the occurrence of the financial effect is contingent upon 
intervening events," must be considered. (Regulation 18701(b)(l).) 
The Arroyo Grande City Council's mere decision to formally support or oppose a potential 
Grover Beach homeless center will not have a reasonably foreseeable financial impact on your real 
property interest in Grover Beach. Arroyo Grande has no decision-making authority with respect to 
the actual development of the homeless center, given that the project site is in another city. Any 
financial effect on your real property, based on an essentially symbolic vote with no direct 
consequences in terms of whether the homeless center is developed or not, would be merely 
hypothetical and entirely contingent upon intervening events. Accordingly, you do not have a 
conflict of interest under the Act and are not disqualified from taking part in the decision of whether 
to formally support or oppose a potential Grover Beach homeless center. 
We emphasize that the above analysis focuses solely on whether you may take part in the 
Arroyo Grande City Council's decision to take a formal, but ultimately symbolic stance on a 
neighboring town's development project. As noted, if the government decision is limited to whether 
Arroyo Grande supports or opposes the homeless center project, the decision would not have a 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on your real property interest and you may take part in the 
decision. 


File No. A-19-076 
Page No. 3 
We caution, however, that if the Arroyo Grande City Council subsequently considers taking 
steps beyond merely voting on a formal stance that may influence the actions of Grover Beach-
then the analysis of whether the decision presents a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect 
to your real property will differ. To the extent that the Arroyo Grande City Council will 
communicate its support or opposition of the project to Grover Beach, it is foreseeable that the 
Grover Beach's decisions regarding the project may have a material financial effect on your 
property. Under Regulation 18704(c)(2), a public official is not permitted to use his or her official 
position to influence a decision before another governmental agency. Under this restriction, you 
may not contact or appear before another governmental agency for the purpose of affecting the 
decision with a foreseeable and material effect on your interests while acting or purporting to act in 
your official capacity. Accordingly, you may not contact or appear before Grover Beach in your 
official capacity in an attempt to influence the project, and the Arroyo Grande City Council should 
not identify you by name or any other reference if it communicates its support or opposition of the 
project to Grover Beach. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division

own residential property approximately 638 feet from the potential site of the project?

QUESTION: As an Arroyo Grande City Councilmember, may you take part in a governmental decision to 
formally support or oppose a neighboring city's development of a homeless center, given that you 
own residential property approximately 638 feet from the potential site of the project?

CONCLUSION: Yes, because the Arroyo Grande City Council's formal support or opposition to a 
neighboring town's homeless center would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on 
your real property interest.
